coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-integrated nanosensor based on N, P co-doped graphene quantum dots for fluorescence detection of acid red 18 in food
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Guoqiang
Qian, Xiaoqing
Li, Tingting
Gao, Sineng
Zhang, Baoli
Wang, Linfan
Liu, Kui
Gu, Chenjie
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Exploring graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that can be done to contaminant residue detection has been widely concerned. Herein, we develop nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, P-GQDs) with a strong green emission (quantum yield: 0.582) by using the resorcinol and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer as precursors via the one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized N, P-GQDs exhibit excellent photostability and excitation-independent characteristic. Meaningfully, N, P-GQDs exhibit good fluorescence response to acid red 18 (AR18) and possess a good linear relationship over the range of 0–50 μM (the detection limit: 27 nM). Meanwhile, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer is considered as the possible sensing mechanism. Furthermore, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform is designed for the detection of AR18, which can realize on-site quantitative analysis and exhibit excellent on-site detection capability (the detection limit: 43 nM). Therefore, the present strategy has a prospective value for the application in the on-site monitoring of AR18.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped
Acid red 18 sensing
Color analysis
Smartphone-based detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of AR18 using N, P-GQDs
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform for on-site AR18 detection

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of N, P-GQDs: (a) Plan-view TEM image of the as-prepared N, P-GQDs. The insets correspond to the HR-TEM image and size distribution histogram of N, P-GQDs. (b) Contact-mode AFM image of the N, P-GQDs. The inset corresponds to the thicknesses of N, P-GQDs. (c) Raman spectrum of N, P-GQDs. (d) Full-scan XPS spectrum of the N, P-GQDs. (e) C 1s XPS spectrum. (f) N 1s XPS spectrum. (g) P 2p XPS spectrum. (h) O 1s XPS spectrum. (i) FTIR spectrum of N, P-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, P-GQDs. The insets are optical images of N, P-GQDs aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (right). (b) PL spectra of N, P-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) The mapping result of the relationship between fluorescence emission wavelength (λem) and excitation (λex). (d) Effects of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs. (e) Variation of fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs under irradiation of 365 nm UV light. (f) Effects of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs.

(a) PL spectra of N, P-GQDs with different concentrations of AR18. (b) The relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of AR18. (c) The linear fitting between F/F0 and the concentration of AR18 within a range from 0 to 50 μM. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of (i) N, P-GQDs solution, (ii) AR18 solution, (iii) N, P-GQDs + AR18 compound aqueous solution and (iv) theoretical curve of curve i + curve ii. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of AR18, and PLE and PL spectra of the N, P-GQDs. (f) PL decay time of N, P-GQDs and N, P-GQDs + AR18 mixed solution. (g) Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism inside of N, P-GQDs.

(a) Schematic of smartphone-assisted analyzer based on N, P-GQDs to detect AR18. (b) The digital images of N, P-GQDs with different AR18 concentrations under 365 nm UV light. (c) Output lightness values and color scale card with various concentrations of AR18. (d) Linear plot of LG/LG0 as a function of AR18 concentration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of N, P-GQDs for the synthesis route and the application in AR18 sensing and color analysis.
Determination of AR18 in real samples using fluorescence and HPLC techniques.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The critical t-value at the 95% confidence level is 2.78.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-integrated nanosensor based on N, P co-doped graphene quantum dots for fluorescence detection of acid red 18 in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Oceanography, Ningbo, 315800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Oceanography, Ningbo 315800, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that can be done to contaminant residue detection has been widely concerned. Herein, we develop nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, P-GQDs) with a strong green emission (quantum yield: 0.582) by using the resorcinol and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer as precursors via the one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized N, P-GQDs exhibit excellent photostability and excitation-independent characteristic. Meaningfully, N, P-GQDs exhibit good fluorescence response to acid red 18 (AR18) and possess a good linear relationship over the range of 0–50 μM (the detection limit: 27 nM). Meanwhile, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer is considered as the possible sensing mechanism. Furthermore, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform is designed for the detection of AR18, which can realize on-site quantitative analysis and exhibit excellent on-site detection capability (the detection limit: 43 nM). Therefore, the present strategy has a prospective value for the application in the on-site monitoring of AR18.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid red 18 (AR18) is a common synthetic dye and widely used in candies, cakes and drinks [1]. For the humans daily dietary standard, 4 mg/kg body weight is the maximum intake of AR18 [2]. And the excessive addition of AR18 will pose a threat to human health due to the toxic azo groups and aromatic ring structures of AR18 [3]. Meanwhile, AR18 possesses potential genotoxicity because of its capacity of insert a fragment of DNA to cause a break [4]. Thus, long-term or excessive intake of AR18 will be harmful to human and even cause cancer. Therefore, it is very important to realize quantitative detection of AR18 for food safety field.

Currently, many methods of AR18 detection, such as liquid/gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), ion-pair liquid chromatography and electrochemical analysis have been used for the quantitative analysis of AR18 in food [3–5]. Nevertheless, these methods still remain problems due to the complex operation, tedious time, high cost and low sensitivity [6,7]. Meanwhile, the requirements of on-site quantitative analysis and visual detection still restrict the development of these methods. Nowadays, the smartphone-based on-site detection is growing rapidly due to its advantages of portability, simplicity and controllability [8,9]. And smartphone has been widely integrated with fluorescence sensors, colorimetric sensor, electrochemical sensors and microscopic bio-imaging [10,11]. Among them, fluorescence analysis method based on the red, green and blue components of images has been much used because of their potential real-time applications in the field of biomedical, environmental and food safety [12–14]. Therefore, developing a novel device that can realize on-site quantitative detection of AR18 by analyzing the color change is of great interest.

Nowadays, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as the novel nanomaterials, have been attracted great interest in the field of fluorescence sensing and pollutant analysis due to their excellent photostability, low toxicity, eco-friendly preparation and abundant precursors compared with conventional luminescent materials [15,16]. As recently demonstrated, the diversified GQDs are exploited for the fluorescence sensing. For instance, Sun et al. prepared carbon dots with cyan emission for l-cysteine and zinc thiazole detection [17]. Bian et al. developed sulfur-doped GQDs to realize the detection of Ag+ [18]. However, most of these GQDs are doped with single atom (N or S). Meanwhile, developing the multi-atoms doped GQDs is beneficial to improve the physicochemical characteristic of GQDs, which is because of the synergistic effect of the multi-atoms in GQDs [19,20]. Generally, nitrogen is the common doped element in GQDs because of their comparable atomic sizes to carbon, as well as their strong bonding strength with carbon atoms [21]. As for phosphorus, it will generate a substitutional defect in sp3-domain of GQDs [22]. Thus, realizing the co-doping of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms is of great interest, which can effectively alter and modulate the physicochemical properties of GQDs. Therefore, it is necessary to develop N, P-GQDs with high quantum yield for sensing and further applications.

Herein, we report a rapid and highly productive strategy for the synthesis of green emission N, P-GQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of 58.2% by using resorcinol and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer via the hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Interestingly, after adding AR18 to the prepared N, P-GQDs solution, the fluorescence of N, P-GQDs is quenched through a strong fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Then, a fluorescence sensor based on N, P-GQDs is established to detect AR18 in the range of 0–50 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) is measured to be as low as 27 nM. Finally, we design a smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on N, P-GQDs for AR18 detection, which can realize on-site quantitative analysis by a color values analysis software. Therefore, the present strategy may offer a promising application prospect in various fields including the food, environment and diagnosis safety monitoring.

Resorcinol, phosphonitrilic chloride trimer, NaOH, NaCl, H2SO4, K2CO3, AgCl, MgSO4, ZnCl2, BaCl2, NiCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, PbCl2, CuCl2, CaCl2, FeCl2, AlCl3, rhodamine 6G (R6G), rhodamine B (RB), basic violel (BV) and ethanol aqueous solution were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and all materials were used without further purification. The deionized water was purified by the Millipore purification system and the electrical resistivity is 18.2 MΩ cm.

Herein, the resorcinol is the carbon source and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer is nitrogen and phosphorus sources. Typically, resorcinol (0.132 g), phosphonitrilic chloride trimer (0.140 g) and absolute ethanol (75 mL) were added into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After thorough mixing, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 20 h to obtain the crude product. Subsequently, the crude product was purified by the centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min), dialysis (retained molecular weight: 100–500 Da, 72 h) and filtration (0.22 μm membrane filter) to obtain the pure N, P-GQDs. Finally, the N, P-GQDs was lyophilized and stored at 4 °C for future use.

The morphologies and size of the N, P-GQDs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100). And the thickness of N, P-GQDs was recorded on atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried by a Japan Kratos Axis Ultra HAS spectrometer, which was used to determine the chemical states and surface chemical composition of N, P-GQDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on a Rigaku Ultima-IV X-ray diffractometer to recognize the crystallinities. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet 6700). Raman spectrum was obtained by the SPL-CX40M with a 532 nm Ar+ laser. The Zeta potentials were analyzed using a Zeta potential analyzer (ZetaPALS, Brookhaven). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption, photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were taken by a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Japan) and a Shimadzu fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Japan), respectively. The high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was measured on the LC-20AT series HPLC system (Shimadzu).

For the quantum yield of N, P-GQDs, the standard sample is quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4, QY: 0.54). The fluorescence QY of N, P-GQDs was measured by the following equation (1),
(1)
φ A
=
φ
S T
×
(
Κ A
/
Κ
S T
)
×
(
η A
2
/
η
S T
2
)
Where φ, K and η are fluorescence quantum yield, the slope of the line formed by the integral area and the ultraviolet absorbance, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscript A indicates the object to be tested, and ST indicates the reference. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance value of solution was fixed at 0.1 at the same excitation wavelength.

For the detection of AR18, 10 μL of solution with various concentrations of AR18 (0–120 μM) was mixed with 100 μL of N, P-GQDs water solution (1 mg/mL). Then the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature after reaction for 3 min. In the selectivity experiments, the N, P-GQDs water solution was added various common ions and dyes (Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Al3+, R6G, RB and BV) to replace AR18 under the same experimental conditions.

The milk, red tea and candy were obtained from the store near Ningbo University. First, 1g candy was crushed and dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. Then, the liquid samples (milk, red tea and candy solution) were purified by filtration (0.22 μm membrane filter) to remove the impurity. Subsequently, 1 mL of solution with different concentrations of AR18 was mixed with 10 mL of the liquid samples for later detection.

The smartphone-assisted sensing platform was equipped with an excitation light source (365 nm), a sample cell and a smartphone holder. Meanwhile, the device was prepared by using 3D printing technology and the exterior materials were all black to reduce the interference of external environment. In addition, the light source and the sample cell were fixed, which will reduce the errors in the detection. For the detection of AR18, different concentrations of AR18 (2 mL) were mixed with 2 mL of N, P-GQDs. Then, the cuvette with mixture will put in the sample cell. Under the 365 nm excitation light, the fluorescence images of mixture will be obtained by the smartphone camera. Finally, the lightness of green (LG) information of images will be recognized by the color-analysis app.

In order to obtain the N, P-GQDs with optimal optical properties, we explore the reaction conditions (reaction temperature, time and the molar ratio of reactants) to prepare N, P-GQDs. As shown in Fig. S1(a), the fluorescent intensity of the prepared N, P-GQDs increases with the increase of the reaction temperature from 160 to 180 °C. As the temperature further increases, such as 200 °C, the fluorescent intensity will decrease. Thus, the influence of reaction time on the fluorescent intensity of N, P-GQDs is studied at 180 °C. For the reaction time, the fluorescent intensity of N, P-GQDs first increases and then decreases, which exhibits the maximum intensity at 20 h (Fig. S1(b)). Therefore, 180 °C and 20 h are set as the optimal reaction temperature and time, respectively. Subsequently, the molar ratio of resorcinol (R) and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer (P) in the preparation of N, P-GQDs is also explored in Fig. S1(c). When the R/P ratio is varied from 1:4 to 1:1 or from 4:1 to 1:1, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs increases first and then decreases (Fig. S1(d)). Interestingly, the emission wavelength exhibits no change in all R/P ratios. Therefore, 3:1 is the optimal reagent ratio.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is characterized to investigate the morphology and size distribution of N, P-GQDs (Fig. 1 (a)). It can be observed that the N, P-GQDs are quasi-spherical shape and possess an excellent monodispersity. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1(a) inset) and the corresponding contour line (Fig. S2(a)) show the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.24 nm, which agrees well with the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [23,24]. As displayed in Fig. S2(b), the fast Fourier transform (FFT) image shows a standard six-fold symmetry. And the average diameter of N, P-GQDs is 3.4 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 1(a) inset). The atomic force microscope (AFM) image of the N, P-GQDs (Fig. 1(b)) shows that the heights of N, P-GQDs are mainly distributed at 0.3–1 nm and the average height is 0.65 nm (Fig. 1(b) inset), suggesting most of N, P-GQDs are about 1–3 carbon layers [23]. Subsequently, Raman spectrum is explored to study the structural feature of N, P-GQDs (Fig. 1(c)). There are two peaks centered nearby 1350 cm−1 (D bands) and 1585 cm−1 (G bands), and the intensity ratio of I
D
/I
G is 0.82, which indicates the N, P-GQDs are almost in crystallized structure. In addition, a broad diffraction peak centered at about 23.4° is observed in XRD pattern (Fig. S3), which further confirms the graphene structure of N, P-GQDs [25].

To investigate the elementary composition and surface functional groups of N, P-GQDs, the XPS spectrum of N, P-GQDs is performed in Fig. 1(d-h). The full scan XPS spectrum of N, P-GQDs in Fig. 1(d) shows that C 1s, N 1s, P 2p and O 1s peaks appeared at 284 eV, 400.5 eV, 133 eV and 532 eV, respectively. And the element content of C, N, P and O are measured to be 71.8%, 3.05%, 1.81% and 23.34%, which indicates the successful doping of N and P in GQDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of N, P-GQDs (Fig. 1(e)) is divided into CC bonds (284.4 eV), C–O/C–P (285.2 eV) and C–N (286.2 eV) [26,27]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) reveals the three binding energies at 399.8 eV, 401.2 eV and 402.1 eV, representing N–H bond, N–P bond and C–N bond [26], respectively. The P 2p spectrum (Fig. 1(g)) can be fitted into two peaks at 132.6 eV and 133.5 eV, which belong to P–O and P–C/P–N bonds [2]. The O 1s spectrum consists of two peaks at 532.2 eV (O–H) and 533 eV (C–O) (Fig. 1(h)). In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1(i)), the broad absorption band at 2916-3420 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, implying that the surface of N, P-GQDs possesses lots of hydrophilic groups. Simultaneously, sharp peaks at 1510 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 962 cm−1 correspond to the CC, C–N and C–P vibrations, respectively [28]. In addition, there are two absorption bands at 1150 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1, which are assigned to P–O and C–O groups [29]. These above results show that N, P-GQDs possess the N and P-related functional groups, which was consistent well with the results of the XPS results.

Subsequently, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the prepared N, P-GQDs are examined. As illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the resultant N, P-GQDs shows an obvious absorption band at 233 nm due to the π-π* transition of conjugated CC bond [30]. Meanwhile, a broad absorption bond at 270 nm is observed, which is derived from the n-π* transition of CO and the excited defect surface states caused by doping of P, N atoms [31,32]. The fluorescence spectra of the N, P-GQDs exhibit the optimal emission at 507 nm under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm (Fig. 2(a)). Meanwhile, the N, P-GQDs solution appear slight yellow under sunlight and show green fluorescence on the illumination with a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2(a) inset). In addition, with the increase of excitation wavelength from 370 to 470 nm, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs increases and then decreases, and it exhibits obvious excitation-independent characteristic in Fig. 2(b). The excitation-emission matrix graph map in Fig. 2(c) shows a single emission center at 507 nm under the 420 nm excitation. Moreover, the QY of N, P-GQDs (Fig. S4) excited with 365 nm was calculated as high as 58.2%.

The stability of fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs against environmental conditions, including pH, UV exposure and storage times, plays a vital role in further applications. As presented in Fig. 2(d), the N, P-GQDs exhibit stable fluorescence when the pH value changes from 3 to 8, and it exhibits a slight decrease when pH reaches 12. This result indicates that neutral conditions are more favorable for fluorescent display. Furthermore, the PL intensity of the synthesized N, P-GQDs shows only a decrease of 4.81% with the continuous illumination of UV light (365 nm) for 180 min (Fig. 2(e)). Finally, as shown in Fig. 2(f), long-term (for one month) stability of the synthesized N, P-GQDs is also explored, which exhibit minor decrease of PL intensity. These results indicate that the N, P-GQDs exhibit outstanding stability of optical properties, which paves the way for further practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AR18 using N, P-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing properties of N, P-GQDs toward different ions and dyes are investigated, such as Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Al3+, AR18, R6G, RB and BV. As shown in Fig. S5, only AR18 can cause obvious fluorescence variation, which clearly indicate that the N, P-GQDs have great specificity for AR18 detection and have potential as a fluorescent probe for the detection of AR18. Subsequently, Fig. 3 (a) exhibits the fluorescence intensity change of N, P-GQDs with the addition of various concentrations of AR18 from 0 to 120 μM. With the concentration of AR18 increases, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs decreases. Then the quantitative data of F/F
0 (F and F
0 is the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs with and without AR18) with AR18 concentration from 0 to 120 μM are shown in Fig. 3(b). It exhibits an excellent linear relationship (R
2 = 0.997) in the range of 0–50 μM with LOD of 27 nM (Fig. 3(c)), which is no more than the maximum intake standard of AR18 in drinks (4 mg/kg, amount to 6.6 μM) [2]. In addition, the fluorescence image of N, P-GQDs with increase of AR18 concentration under the 365 nm excitation is shown in Fig. S6. Compared with the previously reported methods for AR18 detection (Table S1), the N, P-GQDs based probe exhibits the better LOD and detection concentration range in our work.

Subsequently, the sensing mechanism for detecting AR18 is evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the UV–vis absorbances of N, P-GQDs (curve i), AR18 (curve ii), N, P-GQDs/AR18 mixed solution (curve iii) and the sum of curve i + curve ii (curve iv) are revealed. Compared the curve iv with curve iii, it can be seen that curve iii exhibits no displacement of peaks, which indicates there is no complex or covalent bonding formed between N, P-GQDs and AR18 [33]. Therewith, the fluorescence spectra of N, P-GQDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of AR18 are exhibited in Fig. 3(e). It is observed that the broad absorption peak at 503 nm of AR18 has a significant overlap with the emission peak of N, P-GQDs, indicating that quenching of N, P-GQDs by AR18 may be caused by FRET [34]. Whereafter, we explore the Zeta potentials of the N, P-GQDs and AR18 to further verify the FRET mechanism. In Fig. S7, it can be observed that the Zeta potentials of N, P-GQDs and AR18 are +20.7 mV and −8.4 mV. Therefore, the electrostatic attraction will be occurred between the N, P-GQDs with negative charges and AR18 with positive charges [35]. Meanwhile, the energy transfer efficiency (E), Förster distance (R0), the distance between the acceptor and the donor (r) are calculated to confirm the existence of the FRET in the GQDs-AR18 system. These values are calculated by the following:
(2)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
=
R 0 6
R 0 6
+
r 6
(3)
R 0
= 0.211
(
k 2
n
− 4
φ X
J
( λ )
)
1 6
(4)
J
( λ )
=
∫ n ∞
F D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ 4
d λ
where F and F
0 express the intensity of the donor (N, P-GQDs) before and after addition of accepter (AR18); R
0 represents the Förster distance when E = 50%; r denotes the distance between N, P-GQDs and AR18; k
2 is the relative direction in the transition dipoles space of N, P-GQDs and AR18 (k
2 = 2/3); n refers to the refractive index of the medium (n = 1.33); φ
x is the fluorescence quantum yield of N, P-GQDs (0.58); J(λ) represents the spectral overlapping area between the emission of N, P-GQDs and the absorption of the AR18; F
D
(λ) describes normalized fluorescence emission spectrum of N, P-GQDs, ε
A
(λ) is the molar absorption coefficient of AR18, and λ is the wavelength. From equations (2)–(4), it can be calculated that R
0 is 1.67 nm, E is 34% and r is 1.86 nm. R
0 and r values are in the range of 1–10 nm, which are consistent with requirements for FRET mechanism [36]. In addition, the fluorescent lifetime of N, P-GQDs in the absence and presence of AR18 is also explored in Fig. 3(f). It can be seen that the fluorescent lifetime of N, P-GQDs exhibits obvious change before and after mixing with AR18, demonstrating that the quenching results from FRET effect [37]. Meanwhile, the abundant N, P-related groups on the N, P-GQDs surface will provide more electrons, which can promote the energy transfer with AR18 and further quench the fluorescence of N, P-GQDs [38]. Therefore, the schematic diagram of the detection mechanism of AR18 is depicted in Fig. 3(g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform for on-site AR18 detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Nowadays, many detection methods have been developed to realize quantitative detection. But most of these methods cannot satisfy the needs of real-time, on-site, rapid and quantitative detection due to their disadvantages of expensive instruments and tedious procedure. Therefore, the smartphone-assisted sensing platform with simple preparation, rapid procedure and outstanding stability has attracted great attentions in the field of fluorescence detection. Herein, we design a handheld smartphone-assisted sensing platform for real-time, on-site and quantitative detection of AR18. By simple operation of the app (Color Picker) installed in the smartphone, the color information of the photos can be recognized (Fig. 4 (a)). Through the smartphone camera, the fluorescence images of N, P-GQDs with different AR18 concentrations under 365 nm UV light can be shown in Fig. 4(b). Subsequently, the lightness information of the fluorescence images can be recognized and the lightness values of green component are recorded (each experiment is repeated 3 times). Meanwhile, the corresponding color scale cards are fabricated in Fig. 4(c). In addition, the relationship between the ratio of LG/LG
0 values (LG
0 and LG represent the lightness of the fluorescence images of N, P-GQDs before and after the addition of AR18, respectively) and the concentration of AR18 is observed in Fig. 4(d). And a good linear range from 0 to 40 μM is obtained for AR18 detection (LOD = 43 nM).

To assess the practical applicability of the N, P-GQDs, we measure AR18 in milk, red tea and candy samples. Meanwhile, the traditional HPLC method is selected as the reference method to evaluate the feasibility of the fluorescence method for AR18 detection [39]. In addition, the t-test method is used to estimate the reliability. As shown in Table 1 , the percentage recoveries are in the range of 98.2–102.0% and the relative standard deviation in percentage (%RSD) is lower than 2.32, verifying the excellent accuracy and reliability of fluorescence method for AR18 detection in real samples. The t-test values are lower than 2.78, which indicates there are no significant differences between both methods at a 95% confidence level. Therefore, the N, P-GQDs possess great potential in AR18 monitoring in practical systems.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized N, P-GQDs with bright green emission and high photoluminescence via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of resorcinol and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer. Remarkably, the as-prepared N, P-GQDs exhibit a high fluorescence quantum yield of 58.2% with outstanding stability and reliability. Upon the addition of AR18, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-GQDs is remarkably quenched due to FRET, and the N, P-GQDs possess a linear relationship in the range of 0–50 μM with a low LOD of 27 nM. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform with N, P-GQDs is designed for AR18 detection, which exhibit excellent good linear range from 0 to 40 μM with a LOD of 43 nM by analyzing the lightness values. Finally, the N, P-GQDs are used in the detection of AR18 in real samples, indicating excellent practical applicability in AR18 detection. Our proposed strategy has the potential for the feasible choice to expand sensing applications monitoring and environmental pollution analysis in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cap.2023.10.005.
